dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, chest pain, started about 30 minutes ago when I was asleep.
You appear alert and oriented to person, place, and time.
You're alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation.
Your GCS is 15 (E4 V5 M6), which means you are fully awake and aware.
Your airway is patent and self-maintained with no signs of obstruction.
You're breathing at a normal rate, around 20 breaths per minute, and showing no signs of labored breathing.
Your skin is warm, dry, and normal in color with a strong and regular radial pulse.
You have hyperlipidemia and primary hypertension, with a history of an AMI in 2018 and stent placement, as per your chart.
Your chest pain is midsternal, described as squeezing, and radiates to your neck and is worse with palpation.
The pain is currently at a 5 out of 10, down from 10 after taking nitroglycerin but not as effective as usual.
You're on ticagrelor, metoprolol, atorvastatin, lisinopril, apixaban, and nitroglycerin, correct?
You don't have any known drug allergies or a reported history of gastrointestinal bleed.
The pain woke you up from sleep. Have you had any other symptoms like dizziness?
No, just the chest pain and a little discomfort in my neck.
Your pulse is 83, regular.
Your breathing is steady at 20 breaths per minute.
Your blood pressure is 112 over 65.
Your oxygen saturation is 96% on room air, which is normal for you.
The glucose check returned a zero. We think this may be due to your equipment or a fasting state.
The EKG shows a normal sinus rhythm for a male over 40 years old.
We placed a 20G IV in your left wrist with a saline lock for medication access as needed.
We've given you 324mg of aspirin after a two-provider cross-check and confirming no allergy or bleeding risk.
A 4-lead cardiac monitor is running, and a 12-lead ECG was obtained with no STEMI changes.
You asked to walk to the stretcher with a steady gait, and we have secured you for transport.
You're placed in a position of comfort with restraints applied for safe transport.
We have contacted the ER with your updates.
Vitals retaken as 81 pulse, 115 over 72 BP, 97% oxygen saturation, still without arrhythmias.
Confirming that you took one 0.4mg sublingual nitroglycerin, which only partially helped, with no changes since.
You remain alert and oriented with no changes during transport to the hospital.
We followed protocol 2-1 for chest pain with suspected cardiac involvement.
You chose to go to VCU Health Systems Cold. We are en route now.
Everything is secured and we've initiated a final check before entering the expressway.
Vitals remain stable throughout our journey. No distress reported at any point.
No new meds given. ASA effective; pain at 5 and no signs of deterioration.
Winning crew, patient condition unchanged, vitals stable, care is now with the hospital staff.
